Title: The Saddest Song
Comment: In Ireland, someone, like me, who comes from outside the capital, is called a culchie, which derives from the view held by Dubliners that someone who isn't a Dubliner is uncultured. Adrian Crowley is a culchie who lives in Dublin. Will he do? You'll find a reason to downvote him somehow I'm sure. He's supported Eitzel if that means anything. And remember, it's not where you're from it's where you're at.

Title: Worm Grew a Spine
Comment: Hi Flirs - first entry - Elias the Lead Singer of Iceage out of Copenhagen - from his solo record that came out last year - especially the bringe in this song is pure magic.

Title: BIRDS
Comment: These kids have recently blown up worldwide and we are proud to call them. Baltimorons ￼

Title: ما نسيتك ثواني
Comment: Wow, so how do I distill a whole culture into 1 song! I suppose, that's not the purpose. Anyways, so I live in Abu Dhabi, UAE - for those unfamiliar, it's on the straight of H, enough said about all that. This is from an old Ahlam album. You really need to be in the time & place I guess, I think it's probably quite a cultural stretch for many. Hope some of you like it.

Title: Let's Go Out Tonight
Comment: The name of the artist on this recording actually stays in my area in Glasgow-technically Paul Buchanan was never really Glaswegian .  I'm going to talk a little about Glasgow here -I lost one of my greatest friends on Easter Monday . Ironically Gordon was American but moved back to Glasgow in the late 60's-a story that was always told as being due to his parents splitting up. There's a sting in that tale. I will recount on the follow up replies. Me and Gordon met in 1982 when Glasgow was a thriving centre of cool-if that matters but him being 4 years older than me-he took me under his wing and we went to the best bars and clubs. Music was central to us-he wasn't a rock fan per se -and we gelled on The Blue Nile and I turned him onto Eitzel. We went to the football together for over 40 years and he looked after me when I really needed it and was an uniquely good person. I miss him deeply so this is for him. . I miss the Glasgow of this song too.

Title: Interpretes
Comment:
"I wanna go back to my home town
Though I know it'll never be the same"
- Joe Jackson

This is a timely round, as I am actually spending the summer with family in Midland, Michigan (USA). 

Years ago, I was home on break from university, and like many young folks I took a short-term job to make some extra money. I was part of a rotating work crew for our local public works department, doing various tasks like pouring sidewalks, filling potholes, and clearing brush.

One of my supervisors was a friendly man named Ivan. We were at lunch one day when he told a story about going to see his son play in a band opening for Neil Young. It took me a minute, but I figured out he was referring to Steve Shelley of Sonic Youth (who, by the way, is the spitting image of his father).

We've already had SY and current collaborator Bill Orcutt, so here's a track from Shelley's 2009 team-up with Chicago indie rock artists Disappears and White/Light, eventually released on Shelley's own label in 2020.

Title: Love Bird
Comment: Welcome to Snoqualmie, birthplace of Ella Raines (watch "Tall In the Saddle" with John Wayne if you ever get a chance).  Issaquah, the second town west on Interstate 90, would have given you Modest Mouse.  Instead, here's a 2017 offering from Snoqualmie local Strong Sun Moon (an acoustic world-fusion and roots group featuring Camelia Jade and Mike Antone).  The alternate choice was Mother Root, a heavy sludge/doom duo.  You're welcome.

Title: New York, Let's Do Nothing
Comment: I felt sure we’d had King Hannah before, but the spreadsheet says no. My favourite Liverpool band of recent years, an irresistible mix of Mazzy Star, Neil Young and PJ Harvey, with a fondness for chatty but surreal lyrics - shining stars in a city currently with an apparently infinite supply of bands who look and sound like The La’s (if being generous) or Cast (if being honest).

Title: Summer
Comment: These wide-open rounds seem easy at first, but give me indecision paralysis. Melbourne (Australia) has obviously produced a decent band or two, but after a while thinking through some big names, I landed on a more underground moment that still lurks in the city’s musical DNA. This is from a wondrous compilation gathering some of the strangest and spikiest output of the post-punk years from 1978-82, not all of it from Melbourne, but featuring a good number of the Little Bands from our town, a kind of musical swingers’ scene in which groups formed, performed once or twice, then vanished, before reshuffling again (complete with disposable band names like Slugfuckers, People With Chairs Up Their Noses, Thrush and the Cunts). The Take only recorded this one song, but for me it nails the spirit of the era - yelping vocals, a touch of sadism (that siren guitar!), and serious humour .

Title: Shadow (Maceo Plex Remix)
Comment:
Well, I could be one of two Portland fireflies on the list. Betcha can't guess which one I am! The past Firefly music leagues I have posted a sampler plate of the rich music scene we have here in PDX.  However, if you examine the origin of some of those, you will find some of the most well known Portland band members actually are transplants to our fair city. Finding a band whose members were born and bred here was a challenge.  

Here, we have the Chromatics, whose members are all Portland natives. Their sound is gauzy, airy, dripping in reverb, just ripe for the picking to appear on TV and movie soundtracks. This remix, by producer Maceo Plex, was intended to "pay tribute and honour the death of Twin Peaks‘ fictional character, Laura Palmer who was originally portrayed by Sherly Lee."

Portland's creative landscape continues to thrive in spite of efforts by red hats to dry out our dampness. Keep Portland Weird! (Yes, I know we that slogan was stolen from Austin).

Title: Good Life
Comment:
Iggy Pop was raised in a trailer park in my town of Ypsilanti, very close to the grocery store I shop at.  However, Memorial Day weekend in Detroit means it's time for the annual electronic music festival, now called Movement.  This year, on the Friday before the long weekend, I was lucky enough to catch a super rare-- nay, historical-- show by the Belleville Three (Techno originators Juan Atkins, Derrick May and Kevin Saunderson) in their hometown where they met as teens, Belleville, Michigan, which is next door to Ypsilanti.
It is hard now to imagine the world before EDM took it over like a gigantic, tedious octopus.  But these kids (now approaching retirement age) had the skills and vision to create something new and exciting.
Kind of a funny sidenote: 10% of the door take was donated to the Belleville High School robotics club, as befits the futuristic music.
Here is Inner City's (Kevin Saunderson  + singer Paris Grey) 1988 classic "Good Life".  Shake your booty.

Title: Ten Bells
Comment: There are a lot of very famous bands from Liverpool, but I’m sure you’ll all agree the first one that comes to everyone’s mind is these guys banging away at their guitars with mallets. Right?

Title: Info Freako
Comment: Nothing from this small town in Wiltshire but just down the road in Bradford on Avon there was Jesus Jones. A record that appeared to predict the coming of the internet age.

Title: Class War Now
Comment:
Since I can't use the ol'  "little league games in NJ and Subway sandwich" subterfuge to hide my location and identity, I might as well go all in: 

HELP are a tremendously noisy rock band from Portland OR who are best experienced live, and, as this track might suggest, their shows are very politically charged affairs. The drummer doesn't really play the drums, so much as assaults them with an over the shoulder attack (sort of visible in the looping clip on Spotify). Interestingly enough, the guitarist and singer spent time in another far more famous PDX (airport code for Portland which  is common shorthand for the city itself) band, Portugal the Man, which is pretty fascinating when you realize he had all of .... uh....  *this* ...  bottled up inside. Glad he's getting it out!

Title: Go All The Way
Comment:
In the Cleveland metro area, I'm spoiled for choices, but I get overwhelmed by options, so I decided to interpret "the town where you currently live" as narrowly as possible.

Eric Carmen grew up in my current suburb and went to the same high school my kids attend. The city held a celebration of life just after his death.

Although we had another selection from the Raspberries in the last league (and though some voters may take a dim view of repeat artists), this really is an incredible song. I could have opted for one of his later solo outings which were still expertly crafted soft rock hits, but this one is simply better. The absolutely classic opening riff foreshadows the edgy (for the time) subject matter and then transitions almost magically into the most innocent sounding and buttery smooth chorus.

There are plenty of songs with unforgettable hooks and plenty of songs with incredible songwriting, but this one comes from the much smaller pile that offers up both.

Title: Light Night Mountains All That
Comment:
All of my favorite Chicago artists have been represented in one form or another so far. So here’s a new song “about trying to show somebody something cool and its coolness failing to move them beyond indifference or distraction, probably because they are thinking about something or someone else.“

This one comes from their latest album, inspired by the lead singer going to therapy (Americans under 40 really seem to love talking about going to therapy). As such, their album is “Singin’ To An Empty Chair,” a nod to the  Gestalt technique invented in the 1920s. You could DIY, but results may vary.   (https://www.ebsco.com/research-starters/health-and-medicine/empty-chair-technique)

Title: Into the Garden
Album: Into The Garden - An Artery Collection
Comment: Under the radar band from back when. I always loved this but never got a chance to see them until their short lived reformation. That pulsing bass line and textured shards of guitar get me every time. Sheffield if you must know.

Title: Bubble Sex
Comment:
My fellow Dane might kill me for submitting this, but I think it’s time we reexamined the oeuvre of Copenhagen* native mr. Seebach. Primarily known for his somewhat naff Eurovision schlagers (though Disco Tango is a banger; this is a hill I will die on), this song is the best song Giorgio Moroder never wrote. Seebach was an early adopter of electronic music, even if he might primarily have copied trends from abroad. But you can’t deny the excellent result!

* Tommy Seebach was born in Copenhagen but lived in Frederiksberg, which is a municipality nestled entirely within the municipality of Copenhagen. Copenhageners and Frederiksbergians might debate what’s what, and what’s best, but except for tax purposes the rest of the world sees no difference.
